did_this_paper_use_ai_to_obtain_results_.details: The paper analyzes state-of-the-art deep neural network classifiers (specifically Perez et al.'s models and Gessert et al.'s ensemble of EfficientNets) for automatic skin lesion classification (melanoma and seborrheic keratosis) using the ISIC archive. It employs conditional dependence tests based on structural causal models to determine if these AI models use ABCD rule features or bias variables in their decision-making process.

is_this_paper_a_review_paper_or_a_resear.details: The paper presents original experimental results investigating whether state-of-the-art deep neural network classifiers use ABCD rule features or bias variables in automatic skin lesion classification. It employs conditional dependence tests based on structural causal models, conducts sanity checks, and reports findings on specific models (Perez et al., Gessert et al.) using the ISIC archive. Although published in the CVPR Workshops, it contains original methodology application and empirical validation rather than summarizing prior literature.

title: Conditional dependence tests reveal the usage of ABCD rule features and bias variables in automatic skin lesion classification
journal: 2021 IEEE/CVF Conference on Computer Vision and Pattern Recognition Workshops
brief_summary: This study investigates whether state-of-the-art deep neural network classifiers for automatic skin lesion classification rely on medically relevant ABCD rule features or spurious bias variables. The authors applied conditional dependence tests based on structural causal models to analyze models by Perez et al. and Gessert et al. using data from the ISIC archive, specifically the HAM10000 dataset. The evaluation focused on the usage of asymmetry, border, color, and dermoscopic structures, as well as bias features like patient age, sex, skin color, and colorful patches. The results indicate that the classifiers incorporate asymmetry, border, and dermoscopic structures in their decisions but largely ignore the color feature. Furthermore, the analysis revealed that these models heavily rely on bias features, particularly patient age and the presence of colorful patches, rather than solely on the intended clinical features.

lesion_types_studied.details: Primary target class for classification models by Perez et al. [29] and Gessert et al. [14] using the HAM10000 dataset. The study investigates if classifiers use ABCD rule features to distinguish melanoma from benign nevi.
Comparator class to melanoma in the ABCD rule context. Also a specific class label 'NV' in the Gessert et al. [14] ensemble models evaluated on the HAM10000 dataset.
Target class for Perez et al. [29] models and a specific class label 'BKL' in the Gessert et al. [14] ensemble models. The study analyzes if these classifiers use ABCD features or bias variables.
Specific class label 'BCC' in the Gessert et al. [14] ensemble models evaluated on the HAM10000 dataset.
Specific class label 'AK' in the Gessert et al. [14] ensemble models evaluated on the HAM10000 dataset.
Specific class label 'DF' in the Gessert et al. [14] ensemble models evaluated on the HAM10000 dataset.
Specific class label 'VASC' in the Gessert et al. [14] ensemble models evaluated on the HAM10000 dataset.
Specific class label 'SCC' in the Gessert et al. [14] ensemble models evaluated on the HAM10000 dataset.

all_task_types.target: Discrete diagnostic categories (melanoma, seborrheic keratosis, nevi, basal cell carcinoma, actinic keratosis, dermatofibroma, vascular lesion, squamous cell carcinoma)
all_task_types.details: The paper analyzes state-of-the-art deep neural network classifiers for automatic skin lesion classification. Specifically, it evaluates Perez et al.'s models for binary classification tasks (melanoma vs. seborrheic keratosis) and Gessert et al.'s ensemble models for multi-label classification with eight classes plus a rejection class. The study investigates whether these classifiers use ABCD rule features or bias variables in their decision-making process for these discrete classes.

datasets_used.name: HAM10000
datasets_used.description: A dataset from the ISIC archive containing 10,015 images of seven different classes (melanoma, seborrheic keratosis, benign nevi, basal cell carcinoma, actinic keratosis, dermatofibroma, vascular lesion, squamous cell carcinoma) with ground truth segmentations and metadata (patient age, sex). Used for evaluating ABCD rule features and bias features (age, sex, skin color).

use.training: Used as the training dataset for Perez et al. [29] models (ISIC 2017 challenge dataset) and Gessert et al. [14] models.
use.validation: Used for evaluating clinically meaningful ABCD rule features and known bias features (age, sex, skin color).
use.testing: Used for evaluating clinically meaningful ABCD rule features and known bias features (age, sex, skin color).
use.other: Used for evaluating the 'colorful patches' bias feature (first 10,000 images).

datasets_used.name: ISIC 2017 Challenge Dataset
datasets_used.description: The dataset used by Perez et al. [29] for training their models. It is part of the ISIC archive.

use.training: Used for training Perez et al. [29] models for binary classification tasks.

datasets_used.name: BCN20000
datasets_used.description: A dataset of dermoscopic lesions in the wild, used by Gessert et al. [14] for training their multi-label classification ensemble.

use.training: Used for training Gessert et al. [14] models.

datasets_used.name: 2018 ISIC Challenge Dataset
datasets_used.description: A dataset containing 2,594 images with five segmentation masks for each image corresponding to different dermoscopic structures. Used for evaluating the 'dermoscopic structures' feature.

use.testing: Used for evaluating the 'dermoscopic structures' ABCD rule feature.

datasets_used.name: ISIC Archive (SONIC subset)
datasets_used.description: A subset of the ISIC archive, specifically the first 10,000 images which include over 9,000 images from the SONIC dataset (examining nevi in children). Used for evaluating the 'colorful patches' bias feature.

use.testing: Used for evaluating the 'colorful patches' bias feature.

statistical_methods_used.purpose: To test for conditional dependence between features and classifier predictions, specifically to determine if the classifier uses a specific feature. Used to capture linear connections.
Used in conjunction with Partial Correlation to approximate the distribution of coefficients of determination under the assumption of independence and to assess significance.
To check whether one variable can predict another using a decision tree regressor, capable of detecting non-linear relations.
To calculate correlation in a kernel space to account for non-linear relationships between features and predictions.
To determine if a feature is considered 'used' by the classifier based on the results of the conditional dependence tests.

hypothesis_testing.test: Partial Correlation (PC) with shuffle test, Fast Conditional Independence Test (FCIT), and Hilbert-Schmidt Independence Criterion (HSIC) with shuffle test
hypothesis_testing.null_hypothesis: The feature of interest X and the classifier's prediction Y_hat are conditionally independent given the ground truth labels Y (i.e., X ⊥ Y_hat | Y).
hypothesis_testing.alternative_hypothesis: The feature of interest X and the classifier's prediction Y_hat are conditionally dependent given the ground truth labels Y (i.e., X ⫫ Y_hat | Y), implying the classifier uses the feature.
hypothesis_testing.interpretation: A feature is considered 'used' by the classifier if the conditional dependence test reports a significant dependence at the p = 0.01 significance level. For PC and HSIC, significance was determined by comparing the observed test statistic (coefficient of determination or HSIC value) against a distribution generated by shuffling values 1,000 times; if the observed value was larger than 99% of the shuffled values, the dependence was deemed significant.

HAM10000.metric: Conditional Dependence Test Results (Usage of Features)

Perez et al. [29] (Seborrheic Keratosis.dermoscopic_structures_usage: Used by majority of models (implied by 'use the dermoscopic structure' in text, though Table 2 shows mixed results, text summarizes 'use the dermoscopic structure but do not rely on the other features')

Perez et al. [29] (Seborrheic Keratosis.colorful_patches_usage: Used by majority of models (part of 22/23 total)

Gessert et al. [14] (Multi-label Classif.asymmetry_usage: Not used (implied by 'use the border feature' and 'color feature was not used extensively')
Gessert et al. [14] (Multi-label Classif.border_usage: Used by models (text states 'classifiers of Gessert et al. [14] use the border feature')
Gessert et al. [14] (Multi-label Classif.dermoscopic_structures_usage: Detected by HSIC but not PC (nonlinear influence)
Gessert et al. [14] (Multi-label Classif.age_usage: Used by majority of models (part of 20/23 total)
Gessert et al. [14] (Multi-label Classif.sex_usage: Used by few models (part of 4/23 total)
Gessert et al. [14] (Multi-label Classif.colorful_patches_usage: Used by 7/8 Gessert models (part of 22/23 total)

HAM10000.notes: The paper reports that Perez et al. melanoma classifiers use asymmetry and border features. Perez et al. seborrheic keratosis classifiers use dermoscopic structures but not asymmetry or border. Gessert et al. classifiers use border features. Color feature was not used extensively by any group. All classifiers heavily rely on bias features, particularly patient age (20/23 models) and colorful patches (22/23 models).

2018 ISIC Challenge Dataset.metric: Conditional Dependence Test Results (Usage of Dermoscopic Structures Feature)

Perez et al. [29].dermoscopic_structures_usage: Evaluated using this dataset for feature extraction validation

Gessert et al. [14].dermoscopic_structures_usage: Evaluated using this dataset for feature extraction validation

2018 ISIC Challenge Dataset.notes: This dataset (2,594 images with 5 segmentation masks) was used to evaluate the 'dermoscopic structures' ABCD rule feature. The text notes that nonlinear HSIC detects an influence of dermoscopic structures, but linear PC does not, suggesting the feature makes samples difficult to classify but doesn't speak for a specific class.

ISIC Archive (SONIC subset).metric: Conditional Dependence Test Results (Usage of Colorful Patches Feature)
ISIC Archive (SONIC subset).notes: The first 10,000 images from the ISIC archive (including >9,000 from SONIC) were used to evaluate the 'colorful patches' bias feature. The study found that all but one classifier (22/23) use this feature.

Gessert et al. [14].image_id_usage: Used twice (2/92 total instances across all features/models)

Validation Features (Orientation, Random.notes: These features contain little to no meaningful information. The validation experiment confirmed that classifiers do not use these features, with only 2 instances of detected usage out of 92 tests (both for Gessert et al. using Image ID).

architecture_used.main_model: Two sets of models were analyzed: 1) Perez et al. [29] models using ResNet-152, Inception-v4, and DenseNet-161 backbones; 2) Gessert et al. [14] ensemble models primarily based on multi-resolution EfficientNets (specifically EfficientNet-B0 in the authors' reproduction).
architecture_used.input_size: Not explicitly reported in the provided text.

Perez_models.augmentation: Heavy test-time augmentations (specifically augmentation scenario 'J' from Perez et al.). Models aggregated predictions of multiple augmented test examples (e.g., 26 or 64 augmented samples).

Gessert_models.augmentation: Extensive data augmentation and averaging predictions of 36 ordered crops of each example.
Gessert_models.task: Multi-label classification with eight classes and one rejection class.

training_details.hyperparameters: Specific hyperparameter settings were not reported in the text; the authors refer to the original papers [29] and [14] and the authors' provided code for details.

classification_or_output_head.Perez_output: Binary classification (Melanoma or Seborrheic Keratosis) via aggregated predictions.

machine_learning_or_deep_learning_models: Perez et al. [29] models (ResNet-152, Inception-v4, DenseNet-161 backbones with mean and maximum aggregation)
Gessert et al. [14] ensemble models (ensemble of five EfficientNet-B0 models)
preprocessing_techniques: Test-time augmentations (specifically augmentation scenario 'J' from Perez et al.)
Mean aggregation of predictions from multiple augmented test examples
Maximum aggregation of predictions from multiple augmented test examples
Averaging predictions of 36 ordered crops (Gessert et al.)
Principal Component Analysis (PCA) on the top-left 10x10 pixel area for skin color feature extraction
Exclusion of images where the top-left 10x10 pixel area is black (for skin color feature)
Calculation of intersection over union (IOU) for random symmetry feature estimation

data_augmentation_techniques.details: Used augmentation scenario 'J' from Perez et al. [29]. Models aggregated predictions from multiple augmented test examples, specifically 26 or 64 augmented samples, using mean or maximum aggregation.

data_augmentation_techniques.technique: Data augmentation and cropping (Gessert et al.)
data_augmentation_techniques.details: Employed extensive data augmentation during training. During inference, averaged predictions of 36 ordered crops of each example.

conclusions: The conditional dependence method based on structural causal models yields meaningful results for analyzing feature usage in deep neural networks for skin lesion classification.
State-of-the-art classifiers for melanoma (Perez et al.) incorporate asymmetry and border features from the ABCD rule in their decision-making process.
State-of-the-art classifiers for seborrheic keratosis (Perez et al.) incorporate dermoscopic structures but do not rely on asymmetry or border features.
Classifiers by Gessert et al. incorporate the border feature from the ABCD rule.
The color feature from the ABCD rule was not used extensively by any group of classifiers, potentially reflecting an inductive bias in deep neural networks toward shape over color information.

While dermoscopic structures have a nonlinear influence on classification (detected by HSIC), they do not speak for any specific class (not detected by linear PC), suggesting they make samples difficult to classify rather than indicative of a specific diagnosis.
The evaluated classifiers heavily rely on bias features, specifically patient age and the existence of colorful patches, rather than solely on clinically relevant ABCD rule features.

The validation experiments confirmed that the classifiers do not use features with no meaningful information (such as image orientation or random symmetry), with only rare false positives (2 out of 92 tests) for image ID in Gessert et al.'s models.
important_remarks: State-of-the-art classifiers for melanoma (Perez et al.) rely on asymmetry and border features but do not use the color feature, which may reflect an inductive bias in deep neural networks toward shape over color.
Classifiers for seborrheic keratosis (Perez et al.) use dermoscopic structures but do not rely on asymmetry or border features.

The evaluated classifiers heavily rely on bias features, specifically patient age (used by 20/23 models) and the presence of colorful patches (used by 22/23 models), rather than solely on clinically relevant ABCD rule features.
The 'colorful patches' bias is linked to the SONIC dataset, where images often contain large colorful patches associated with benign nevi, potentially allowing classifiers to take shortcuts.

While dermoscopic structures have a nonlinear influence on classification (detected by HSIC), they do not speak for any specific class (not detected by linear PC), suggesting they make samples difficult to classify rather than indicative of a specific diagnosis.

The study validates the conditional dependence method by showing that classifiers do not use features with no meaningful information (e.g., image orientation, random symmetry), with only rare false positives (2 out of 92 tests) for image ID.
The analysis is limited to two specific state-of-the-art model architectures (Perez et al. and Gessert et al.) and may not generalize to all automatic skin lesion classification systems.
The paper highlights that datasets like ISIC may contain unknown biases, and current classifiers exploit these spurious correlations, raising concerns about clinical trustworthiness and deployment.
